subject: Chemistry
title: Potassium: Bananas and Fireworks
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Potassium: Bananas and Fireworks.

hook.text: Potassium explodes in water even more violently than sodium. It also makes bananas slightly radioactive.

buildup.text: Humphry Davy isolated it in 1807 by electrolysis. He was so excited he danced around the lab.

discovery.text: Potassium burns with a violet flame—that's why it's used in purple fireworks.

twist.text: Bananas contain potassium-40, a radioactive isotope. You'd need 10 million bananas at once to get a lethal dose.

climax.text: Your heart depends on potassium. Too much or too little can stop it beating.

punchline.text: Violet flames, radioactive bananas, and your heartbeat. That's potassium.

quiz.question: Why are bananas slightly radioactive?
options: They contain potassium-40, a radioactive isotope
They contain trace amounts of uranium
